Okay, the dangers of promoting. I guarantee you that if you own a business, you will come across these at one point or another. If you have not, then you are a weirdo. Okay, so one, have you ever interacted or worked with a company and then you've seen someone else in a position and you've thought to yourself, like how the heck did this dude get this job? Pretty sure we've all been there, we've walked into a place, we've talked to someone, we've been like, I have no idea how they think this person is qualified to do this role. Right? So you would think with how obvious the dangers are that these things would be avoided, but they are not. So that being said, you need to ingrain these concepts in your head so that over time, they're not things that you have to read, apply, but it's how you make decisions. These concepts become so ingrained into you that you filter all decisions you make within your team through these concepts. Okay? So danger number one is the Peter principle. Okay, this theory was developed by Lawrence J. Peter, long time ago. And basically, it basically states that in a hierarchy, in an organization, members are basically promoted so long as they work competently, right? And so what that means is that sooner or later, they're promoted to a position where they're no longer competent. And therefore they remain there, being unable to learn or earn a further promotion. Okay? So you take someone who's a trainer, promote them to head trainer. Head trainer, suddenly they start dropping the ball. And then it's like, all right, clearly they're never going to move up. That person gets frustrated. Over time, you're like, uh, I shouldn't have promoted them there. Now they're sucking because they've lost self-belief, et cetera, right? Peter principle. And the fact of the matter is, is that this is unavoidable because there is literally no way to know at what point someone's going to hit their level of incompetence, right? And so you can continue to promote someone and it could be that the first time you promoted them, it went awesome and you're super excited. And then you promote them to the next level after that. And they just like perform like crap. And so what you have to understand is like, this is a yin and a yang. Like you have to be very mindful of the fact that these things exist, but you also have to know that you'll never going to fully know. Okay? And that's okay. It's not black and white. Danger number two, this one is much more easy to avoid. Okay? The law of crappy people. Okay? So basically what this states is that in any level of an organization, the talent on that level will eventually match the crappiest person with the type. Okay? So other employees with lower titles then naturally benchmark themselves against the crappiest person. Okay? So let me give you an example. Say your admin sees how crappy your ops manager is, right? She sees her performance actually outweighs your ops manager and then she comes to you and is like, I'm demanding a promotion because I outperform this girl. Look at my stats, look at her stats, right? Kind of sucks. So the quickest way to lose respect for your team is to keep someone who's incompetent in the place that they're at because it discourages A players and also misinforms those below that level because they think that is what they need to aim for and that they only need to do this little amount of work to be able to get a promotion, right? And so the natural question is, how do we avoid these things? Honestly, you're going to have a level of self-awareness to a certain extent, but I find it's much more helpful to get outside perspective, okay? So what I call this is like a promotion council. So when you want to promote someone, basically you submit that teammate or employee to review by the other leaders. So say you have, you could just have one other person that you could discuss this with, it doesn't matter. You just need another set of eyes, okay? And then that person or these people will compare that person's skills, the person you want to promote, with the skills the job requires, okay? A lot of the time, you're very invested in promoting someone. Therefore, you are biased to the positives that they have and that they bring to the table. And those who are not as invested in promoting this person, like they're not gonna have to continue doing the work, et cetera, they're going to tell you the real shit and they're not gonna sugarcoat it. And so it's much more helpful to have an outside pair of eyes in these situations.
